one_line_takeaway: Baking safety into LLM pretraining via safety-filtered/recontextualized data, native-refusal and moral-education corpora, and a harmfulness-tag token (with tag-penalizing Safe Beam Search) cuts attack success from 38.8% to ~8% and, unlike post-hoc alignment, survives benign finetuning without degrading general benchmark performance.
key_finding: Safety pretraining substantially and durably reduces attack success rate with no measurable loss on general benchmarks. Headline: ASR drops from 38.8% (standard pretraining) to 8.3-8.4% (safety pretraining + SafeBeam) in the post-benign-finetuning attack setting. On base models (no alignment yet), standard pretraining shows 44% ASR versus ~11% for the safety-pretrained model. Critically, after safety instruction tuning ALL variants look near-zero ASR, but under benign GSM8K finetuning the standard-pretrained model's ASR nearly quadruples back up (~38.8%) while safety-pretrained models stay low, empirically supporting the 'alignment is not unlearning' thesis. The ablation shows that filtering to only the safest data HURTS (raw 38.8% -> score-0-only 43.8%), and that each constructive intervention helps: +rephrasing 33.6%, +native refusal 25.1%, +moral education 23.0% (Fig 4 label; the accompanying text states 20.0% as the final value). Harmfulness-tag applied during PRETRAINING gives ~8% ASR post-benign-FT versus ~30% (roughly 4x worse) when the same tag is applied only during instruction finetuning. A 5% tag injection rate beat 3% and 10%.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'safety pretraining': a data-centric framework that builds safety into an LLM during pretraining rather than via post-hoc alignment, motivated by the authors' central thesis that 'alignment is not unlearning' (once unsafe patterns are absorbed during pretraining they are hard to remove). The paper's OWN framework has four interventions: (i) Safety Filtering via a trained safety classifier that scores webdata on a 0-5 risk scale; (ii) Synthetic Recontextualization, which rephrases potentially harmful data into safe, context-rich narratives instead of discarding it (released as the SafeWeb 100B-token corpus); (iii) Native Refusal training via two new datasets, RefuseWeb (synthetic refusal dialogues built from score 4-5 harmful data) and Moral Education (educational articles conveying the ethical reasoning behind harm); and (iv) Harmfulness-Tag annotated pretraining, which injects a special <potentially unsafe content> token at ~5% of positions in unsafe segments, plus a decoding-time 'Safe Beam Search' algorithm that penalizes beams likely to emit that tag. Deliverables include the SafeLM 1.7B natively-safe model, the released datasets/classifier on Hugging Face, a new 'Base Model Safety Evaluation' (completion-style jailbreak prompts, jb-completions), and a proposed 'Data Safety Report Card' standard for dataset releases. Note: RLHF/DPO/Constitutional AI, Korbak et al.'s conditional pretraining, FineWeb filtering, and prior special-token/refusal-token work (Xhonneux 2025, Jain 2024, Korbak 2023) are CITED prior work, not this paper's contribution; the paper's novelty is extending token-tagging and refusal supervision into the pretraining phase and combining them data-centrically.
threat_model: Adversaries who elicit harmful/toxic content from LLMs. Concretely the paper evaluates three attack surfaces: (1) base (pre-alignment) models completing unsafe prompts in completion style; (2) adversarial jailbreaks via learned adversarial suffixes (GCG-style, Zou et al. 2023) optimized on 100 JailbreakBench instances; and (3) the erosion of alignment through benign (non-adversarial) finetuning, e.g. finetuning on GSM8K, which the paper treats as the primary stress test because post-hoc safety alignment is brittle under it. The paper argues post-hoc alignment (RLHF/DPO/instruction-tuning) gives only a 'false sense of safety' that collapses under benign finetuning, whereas safety baked into pretraining is more durable. A stated limitation of the threat model: the evaluated adversarial suffixes are NOT aware of the SafeBeam inference algorithm, so robustness of SafeBeam against inference-aware attacks is untested.
limitations: (1) Only a single 1.7B-parameter scale is tested; no evidence the results hold at larger scales. (2) The adversarial-suffix jailbreak evaluation is not adapted to SafeBeam, so SafeBeam's robustness against inference-algorithm-aware attacks is unknown (acknowledged by the authors). (3) Post-benign-FT robustness is demonstrated mainly for benign finetuning on GSM8K; generality across other finetuning distributions is limited. (4) A modest over-refusal cost is introduced by SafeBeam (slight increase in refusals on benign Alpaca requests). (5) Ground-truth safety labels depend on GPT-4/GPT-4o-mini judgments and an LLM judge, inheriting their biases; the safety taxonomy is borrowed from MLCommons/JailbreakBench. (6) A numeric inconsistency exists in the paper itself: the moral-education final ASR is reported as 23.0% in Figure 4 but 20.0% in the ablation text.

datasets_used: FineWeb / FineWeb-Edu (source webdata for scoring, rephrasing, and refusal generation)
SmolLM2 initial corpus components: StackOverflow, FineMath, Cosmopedia (pretraining mixture)
SafeWeb (paper's own 100B-token synthetic recontextualized corpus, released)
Standard benchmarks: ARC-Challenge, ARC-Easy, CommonsenseQA, GSM8K, OpenBookQA, PIQA, TriviaQA, Winogrande, MMLU
Classifier-training toxic sources: 4chan (3,000), ToxiGen (3,000), Jigsaw Toxic Comments (3,000), FineWeb (30,000)
jb-completions (paper's own released base-model completion-style safety eval)
quantitative_results: Attack success rate reduced from 38.8% (standard pretraining) to 8.3-8.4% (safety pretraining + SafeBeam) post benign finetuning
Base-model ASR: 44% (standard PT) vs ~11% (safety PT); ~4x reduction
Ablation ASR (post benign-FT on GSM8K): raw 38.8%, score-0-only 43.8%, +rephrasing 33.6%, +native refusal 25.1%, +moral education 23.0% (Fig 4) / 20.0% (text)
Harmfulness-tag during pretraining ~8% ASR vs harmfulness-tag only during instruction finetuning ~30% (~4x worse)
Tag injection rate ablation: 5% of sequence positions gave lowest ASR (vs 3% and 10%)
Safe Beam Search discards the 50% of beams with highest harmfulness-tag probability at each step
Harmfulness tag injected at ~5% of input sequence length; ~10% harmfulness-tag-annotated WildGuardMix completions added to IFT
Safety classifier trained on 10K GPT-4-annotated samples; thresholds refined with ~600K bootstrapped scored samples
Standard-benchmark averages nearly unchanged: Raw Data 42.8% vs +Refusal+Moral Ed 42.9% (Table 1), confirming no general-capability degradation
baselines_compared: Standard Pretraining (raw webdata) + post-hoc safety instruction tuning
Harmfulness-tag applied at pretraining vs harmfulness-tag applied only at instruction finetuning (Xhonneux 2025 / Jain 2024 / Korbak 2023 style)
Implicit comparison to post-hoc alignment methods (RLHF, DPO, Constitutional AI) cited as brittle
